summary: Drew DeVault sharply criticizes DigitalOcean's Hacktoberfest event, calling it 'Spamtoberfest' and framing it as a corporate-sponsored DDoS attack on open source maintainers. He argues the event incentivizes low-effort spam pull requests that waste maintainers' time, while both DigitalOcean and GitHub deflect responsibility onto the very maintainers being harmed. DeVault contends that Hacktoberfest has never produced meaningful value for open source and exists solely as a marketing stunt. He advocates instead for structured mentorship programs like Google Summer of Code that actually develop software engineers.
key_points: Hacktoberfest incentivizes mass spam pull requests, functioning as a DDoS attack on open source maintainers
Both DigitalOcean and GitHub pass responsibility for dealing with spam entirely onto maintainers
Intrinsically motivated contributors produce far more valuable work than those incentivized by T-shirts
Corporate-sponsored 'social coding' platforms produce Facebook-like engagement problems when applied to open source
Structured mentorship programs like GSoC are far more effective at growing the open source community
money_quotes: A Digital Ocean-sponsored and GitHub-enabled Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attack is ongoing, wasting the time of thousands of free software maintainers with an onslaught of meaningless spam.
So, according to these two companies, whose responsibility is it to deal with the spam that they've created? The maintainers, of course! All for a T-Shirt.
Hacktoberfest has never generated anything of value for open source. It's a marketing stunt which sends a deluge of low-effort contributions to maintainers, leaving them to clean up the spam.
Hacktoberfest is, and has always been, about one thing: marketing for Digital Ocean.
When you build the Facebook of coding, you get the Facebook of coding.
We don't need to give away T-Shirts to incentivize drive-by drivel from randoms who will never get any closer to open source than a +1/-1 README.md change.
Programs where a marketing department spends $5,000 on T-Shirts to flood maintainers with garbage and clothe people in ads are doing the opposite: hurting open source.
key_insight: Corporate gamification of open source contribution incentivizes spam and exploitation of maintainers rather than genuine community building.
